title: Spaghetti Aglio e Olio

subs.name: thin wheat noodles or rice vermicelli
subs.notes: cook time will differ; adjust accordingly

subs.name: good-quality refined sunflower or rice-bran oil
subs.notes: flavour will be milder; add a small knob of butter for richness

subs.name: 1 tsp red chilli flakes or 2 fresh green chillies, sliced
fresh coriander leaves
subs.notes: adds a slightly different herbal character but works well in an Indian kitchen

steps.text: Bring a large pot of generously salted water to a boil. Add the spaghetti and cook until 1 minute shy of al dente. Reserve 200 ml of pasta water before draining.

While the pasta cooks, warm the olive oil in a wide pan over the lowest heat. Add the garlic slices and let them steep and turn pale golden over 4–5 minutes — they should sizzle gently, not spit. Remove from heat if they colour too fast.
Add the crumbled dried chillies to the pan and return to low heat for 30 seconds to bloom the flavour. The oil will take on an orange-red tint.
Add the drained pasta directly to the pan. Toss over medium heat, pouring in pasta water a splash at a time, until the oil emulsifies into a light, glossy coating on every strand — about 2 minutes.
Take off heat, scatter in the parsley and a grind of black pepper, toss once more and serve at once.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: pasta → thin wheat noodles or rice vermicelli (cook time will differ; adjust accordingly); parsley → fresh coriander leaves (adds a slightly different herbal character but works well in an Indian kitchen); olive-oil → good-quality refined sunflower or rice-bran oil (flavour will be milder; add a small knob of butter for richness); dried-red-chilli → 1 tsp red chilli flakes or 2 fresh green chillies, sliced (fresh chillies give sharper heat)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A standard kadai or frying pan works perfectly for the garlic oil. Use extra-light olive oil (sold at D-Mart and Nature's Basket) or refined sunflower oil. If spaghetti is unavailable, Ching's or any thin wheat noodle is a convincing substitute. Coriander can replace parsley. The dish is naturally budget-friendly and makes an excellent bachelor or hostel-room dinner.
